title: Potato focaccia
subtitle: Soft, dimpled focaccia enriched with mashed potato
excerpt: An Italian-style focaccia enriched with mashed potato in the dough: the potato keeps the crumb exceptionally soft and moist, and the high hydration gives an open texture. Topped with flaky salt and olive oil.
sourceNotes: Potato-enriched flatbreads are a tradition across northern Italy, particularly in Liguria and Puglia, where local potato varieties are added to bread doughs to extend freshness and soften texture. Public-domain Italian household manuals of the late nineteenth century document the addition of cooked potato to bread doughs as a cost-extending measure that also improves keeping quality. The focaccia format here follows the Ligurian style documented in early-twentieth-century Italian baking records.

content.text: Adding mashed potato to focaccia dough has two practical effects: it raises the hydration without making the dough unmanageably wet, and it keeps the baked crumb soft for longer than a plain flour dough. The potato needs to be fully cooked, riced or mashed very smooth, and cooled before it goes into the dough. A lumpy potato leaves lumps in the bread.

attrs.prepNote: plus extra for the tin and surface

content.text: Combine the flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Add the cooled mashed potato, 30 ml of the olive oil, and the lukewarm water. Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 8 minutes. The dough will be softer and stickier than a standard bread dough; wet hands help more than flour for managing it. Cover and leave for 1 hour until nearly doubled.

Pour 20 ml of olive oil into a lined rectangular tin (roughly 30 × 20 cm). Tip the dough into the tin and use oiled fingers to spread it toward the edges. It may not reach the corners immediately; leave it 15 minutes, then coax it out again. Cover and prove for 30 to 40 minutes. Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional.
Drizzle the remaining 10 ml of oil over the surface and push

holes across the entire surface with all your fingers, going almost to the base. The oil will pool in the holes. Scatter flaky salt over the top. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the top is golden-brown and the edges are crisp. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then transfer to a rack.
